title: Operating Cost of an Evangelion Unit
display_title: Operating Cost of an Evangelion Unit
wikitext:
[]: Operating Cost of an Evangelion Unit?             Quote Post by EVAfacepalm » Mon Mar 04, 2013 4:50 pm Just something I thought of recently. We're repeatedly informed that the EVAs have a huge price-tag. Does anyone have any guesses to the cost of actually operating the thing? We'll leave out the price of actually making it, since there's no economic basis to guess on the cost of making an 80 meter tall cyborg clone covered in power armor. I just want to see if we can get a rough guess as to costs of say; the power, ammo, and collateral damage repairs.

[]:             Quote Post by Atropos » Mon Mar 04, 2013 5:21 pm I believe some Japanese scientist estimated the cost of running a Gundam to be ~72,000,000 dollars. I'd imagine an Eva is at least three times that, and that would be absurdly low.

[]:             Quote Post by Catamari » Mon Mar 04, 2013 10:38 pm This sounds like a fun calculation. Just for the batteries I will assume that NERV has similar battery technology to what we have, now. I will also assume that the Eva batteries are rechargeable. Finally, I will assume that an Eva has a mass of about 1 gigagramme (roughly the mass of a fully prepared space shuttle conduit, fuel and all) and that the batteries constitute about 30% of that, so, 300 megagrammes (about 30 school buses, load to capacity). The most energy dense rechargeable cells we have are Lithium-Ion cells, which have an energy density of 0,72 megajoules per kilogramme. At 300 megagrammes, that's 216 gigajoules of energy (roughly equivalent to the energy in 36 barrels of oil, all exploding at once). Now, we know that Unit-01 will use up it's batteries in about 5 minutes of activity (differs with plot, but Ritsu did state 5 minutes in "Minimal Activity Gain Mode" and 1 minute in full activity, the 5 minute metric is more common). That means it is using about 72 megawatts of power (1 watt equals 1 joule per second, therefore, 216000 megajoules divided by 3000 seconds). That means, it costs costs roughly $8352,00 per second to run an Evangelion (that's using Florida's price of electricity, as I could not find info from Japan, but I imagine it would be something similar, likely a little more). Given that, for Unit-01 to operate the full five minutes, it costs NERV $25.056.000,00.

[]:             Quote Post by user-02 » Mon Mar 04, 2013 10:40 pm The US Navy estimates that running and maintaining a nuclear aircraft carrier costs about $500,000 a day, or roughly $182.5 million per annum. And let's consider an Evangelion at least an order of magnitude more costly than that. I think a highly conservative estimate would be $10 billion per unit per year. And that's not accounting for the cleaning up and rebuilding of property that seems necessary after every battle with an angel.

[]:             Quote Post by A.T. Fish » Tue Mar 05, 2013 7:44 am Don't forget the conversion to yen, the numbers will be even more ridiculous.

[]:             Quote Post by Stryker » Tue Mar 05, 2013 2:57 pm It was enough to bankrupt Canada. . . .You know what? Fuck Evas.

[]:             Quote Post by driftking18594 » Tue Mar 05, 2013 3:15 pm Atropos wrote: I believe some Japanese scientist estimated the cost of running a Gundam to be ~72,000,000 dollars. I'd imagine an Eva is at least three times that, and that would be absurdly low.

[]:             Quote Post by EVAfacepalm » Tue Mar 05, 2013 4:08 pm Does anyone here know the cost of manufacturing a single bullet? So we can scale that up to get the cost of ammo for the weapons. Which ironically enough, never work.

[]:             Quote Post by Squigsquasher » Tue Mar 05, 2013 4:23 pm Let's be fair on the pallet rifles, they do work, as long as the AT field has been neutralized first. Look what happened to Matarael. As for the operation costs...through the firetrucking roof. I imagine the only reason NERV can afford it is because they more or less own the government, and the alternative to not giving them unlimited funds is nasty angelic death. In other words, they'd never get away with it in America. I imagine some politician yelling something about "THAT PURPLE PAINT IS A WASTE OF THE TAXPAYER'S DOLLARS!!!" shortly before Sachiel tells him to shut up, steps on him, apologizes for the mess, and promptly leaves to find a country more grateful for Kaiju invasion.

[]:             Quote Post by EVAfacepalm » Tue Mar 05, 2013 4:26 pm Hey... that's another thing. NERV is already essentially bankrupting every country in the UN. Why did they have to paint the Evangelions again? Paint that can stand up to that kind of punishment and still survive has got to be expensive.

[]:             Quote Post by Stryker » Tue Mar 05, 2013 4:32 pm ^^^Let's work off of the standard NATO 5.56x45mm round, which is the most commonly used round in Japan, due to the service rifle of Japan being the Howa Type 89 , and to a lesser extent, the Type 64 (which uses the NATO 7.62x51, but is being replaced by the Type 89). If the bullet is made of tungsten, then it will be 32 cents; If it is leaded, it is 21 cents. We cannot scale the cost of a bullet to that of a "Eva bullet" (thy shall be dubbed) directly, though. Rather, if we want to make a remotely accurate assumption, we will have to: 1. Find the mass of an Eva. 2. Find the proportion of the mass of an Eva to, say, a human in modern ballistics armour. 3. Find the mass of a tungsten bullet (approximately 4.99 grammes) 4. Apply the proportion that we found from Eva to human, to bullet to "Eva Bullet" to find the mass of an Eva bullet (which may not be accurate) 5. Find how much said Eva bullet costs, in accordance to it's mass. Depending on whether we can actually find the mass of an Eva or not, we might have to work off of it's volume, which may lead to an even less accurate assumption.

[]:             Quote Post by EVAfacepalm » Tue Mar 05, 2013 4:44 pm Could you figure out the mass if you had a caliber? Rebuild's Pallet Rifle is stated to fire 209 mm shells. This seems small given the same movies showing the casings to be the size of a car, but whatever. Also, wouldn't the amount of propellant need to be taken into consideration?

[]:             Quote Post by Stryker » Tue Mar 05, 2013 5:01 pm Are we looking at the bullet, or the round? The round, we do have to take propellant into consideration, but for the bullet, we do not. 209mm shells are not that small; A battleship shell is about 280mm, not that much larger. And we probably could find out the mass. . . We should use the proportion of the battleship shell's weight (300) kg to it's diameter (280mm), apply the proportion to the 209mm shells, and then convert it into mass. It would kind of look like this: 300/280 = x/209. X = to an irrational number (223.9285714. . .), So we'll say it weighs about 224 Kg. Lucky for us, the metric system is already in mass, so the mass is 224 Kg. In industry, 1 Kg of Tungsten costs $35 (According to this guy , so take it with a grain of salt), so 224 Kg of tungsten would be worth $7840, meaning an individual round is $7840. If only if it were that easy. A shell isn't a large piece of metal shaped into a bullet. It's got many components, and it is dependent on the type of shell it is. Based off of the fact that the shells seem to explode on contact, we'll say it is an HE-shell. They have a case, a bursting charge, and a fuse. This means that we will have to find all the proportions of size per weight for everything, except the fuse (I wouldn't think the size would matter for the fuze, because even a little flame can spark up a large explosion). We'd go through the same steps as said in the previous paragraph two times, for two things we don't know the exact weight for. This is kind of complicated.

[]:             Quote Post by EVAfacepalm » Tue Mar 05, 2013 5:16 pm So we'll just round up and say $10,000 (US) per round. No wonder Misato was upset when Shinji went full auto on Shamshel. All in all, even if the Angels were defeated and any kind of Third Impact prevented, the world would be in one of the worst economic recessions ever just from the cost of the Evas. Also, the collateral damage. How much is a skyscraper worth again? Especially one designed to retract into the ground.

[]:             Quote Post by Stryker » Tue Mar 05, 2013 5:20 pm EVAfacepalm wrote: The world would be in one of the worst economic recessions ever just from the cost of the Evas.

[]:             Quote Post by Catamari » Tue Mar 05, 2013 5:56 pm Although an Eva would likely not have an in-built nuclear reactor, I think it is a safe assumption that NERV HQ would have one or two. Uranium reactors can produce a massive amount of energy per kilogramme of fuel. We do know that NERV has backup diesel generators, and that they are powerful enough to keep the Magi running. Then again, for all we know, NERV may have a secret underground room with billions of squirrels running on wheels.

[]:             Quote Post by KingXanaduu » Tue Mar 05, 2013 6:09 pm Stryker wrote: All in the name of [s]science[/s] humanity. It was all worth it.

[]:             Quote Post by riffraff11235 » Tue Mar 05, 2013 6:10 pm Catamari wrote: Then again, for all we know, NERV may have a secret underground room with billions of squirrels running on wheels.

[]:             Quote Post by InstrumentalityOne » Tue Mar 05, 2013 8:36 pm Just so you guys know, giving a Boeing a paintjob alone already costs 500,000€ An Eva is roughly 2 times longer and has more limbs to paint, so the paintjob alone would run you for 2 Million €. And that's just for the cosmetic layer.

[]:             Quote Post by Minaplo » Wed Mar 06, 2013 12:14 am Oh good Lord, the costs... Alright, inspired by Catamari's work on the Batteries, I decided to look at the Positron Sniper Rifle used to take down Ramiel in Episode 6. Hyuga informs Misato that they need 'at least' 180 million kilowatts to pierce Ramiel's AT Field. Using Japanese rates (which can go from 5-100 yen per kilowatt hour,) we get an overall price range of: Minimum estimate (5y): 900,000,000 yen (9.6 million US) per shot Maximum estimate (100y): 18,000,000,000 yen ( 193.1 million US !) per shot The real cost is likely to be somewhere in the middle (about 50-100m US), and is likely to be affected by Eva-timeline technological advances in technology that might differ from our own. In any case that's still an absurd figure to be throwing around.

We'll leave out the price of actually making it, since there's no economic basis to guess on the cost of making an 80 meter tall cyborg clone covered
